en.extract:
Saunders's tern (Sternula saundersi), sometimes known as the black-shafted tern, is a species of bird in the family Laridae.
It is sparsely resident along the shores of the north-western Indian Ocean (namely southern Somalia, Arabian Peninsula, Socotra, Pakistan, Andaman and Nicobar Islands and northern Sri Lanka).
It is a close relative of the little tern, least tern, yellow-billed tern, and Peruvian tern. In non-breeding plumages, the Saunder's tern and the little tern may be almost indistinguishable. The two have been considered by some to be conspecific.


Description
Saunders's tern shares many of the features of its close relative, the little tern, including a white body, a black-tipped yellow bill, and a black cap with a white forehead patch. In its breeding plumage, it distinguishes itself with a straight-bordered forehead patch, possessing more black outer primaries, and duskier colored legs and feet in comparison to the bright yellow legs of the little tern. In non-breeding plumage, the two are very similar.
Saunders's tern is 20–28 cm (8–11 in) long, weighs 40–45 grams (1.4–1.6 oz), and possesses a wingspan of 50–55 cm (20–22 in). It has an average wing length of 16.6 cm (6.5 in), an average bill length of 2.8 cm (1.1 in), and a tail fork depth of about 2.8 cm (1.1 in) on average.


Taxonomy
Saunders's tern was first described in 1877 by British botanist and ornithologist Allan Octavian Hume during his stay in British India as part of the Imperial Civil Service. It was originally given the name black-shafted ternlet (Sterna saundersi) by Hume, which was later changed to its present name.
It is considered by some to be a subspecies of Sternula albifrons, potentially of a larger species group, or otherwise conspecific. 


Behavior


Feeding
The majority of the Saunders's tern's diet consists of various marine animals, including small fish, crustaceans, and mollusks. It will also eat several varieties of insects. An individual will hover above the water for a sizable amount of time, before making a plunge dive to catch fish.


Status
It is a widely distributed species, and although population numbers are estimated to be small to large and even showing signs of decline, the species is not assessed as being close to the threshold of vulnerability, and its conservation status has been assessed as "least concern" on the IUCN Red List.


References

'''Saunders's tern''' ('''''Sternula saundersi'''''), sometimes known as the '''black-shafted tern''',<ref>{{Cite web|title=Saunders's Tern (Black-shafted Tern) – ''Sternula saundersi'' |url=https://laji.fi/en/taxon/MX.27823/taxonomy |website=Finnish Biodiversity Information Facility}}</ref> is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Laridae]].
It is sparsely resident along the shores of the north-western [[Indian Ocean]] (namely southern [[Somalia]], [[Arabian Peninsula]], [[Socotra]], [[Pakistan]], [[Andaman and Nicobar Islands]] and northern [[Sri Lanka]]).

It is a close relative of the [[little tern]], [[least tern]], [[yellow-billed tern]], and [[Peruvian tern]]. In non-breeding plumages, the Saunder's tern and the little tern may be almost indistinguishable. The two have been considered by some to be [[conspecific]].<ref name=":0" />

== Description ==
Saunders's tern shares many of the features of its close relative, the [[little tern]], including a white body, a black-tipped yellow bill, and a black cap with a white forehead patch. In its breeding [[plumage]], it distinguishes itself with a straight-bordered forehead patch, possessing more black outer [[Flight feather|primaries]], and duskier colored legs and feet in comparison to the bright yellow legs of the little tern.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|last1=Gochfeld|first1=Michael|last2=Burger|first2=Joanna|last3=Kirwan|first3=Guy M.|last4=Garcia|first4=Ernest|date=March 4, 2020|title=Saunder's Tern|url=https://birdsoftheworld-org.proxy3.library.mcgill.ca/bow/species/sauter2/cur/introduction|website=Birds of the World}}</ref> In non-breeding plumage, the two are very similar.

Saunders's tern is {{cvt|20–28|cm|in|0}} long, weighs {{convert|40-45|g}}, and possesses a [[wingspan]] of {{cvt|50-55|cm|in|0}}.<ref name=":0" /> It has an average wing length of {{cvt|16.6|cm|in|round=0.5}}, an average [[Beak|bill]] length of {{cvt|2.8|cm|in}}, and a tail fork depth of about {{cvt|2.8|cm|in}} on average.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Chandler|first1=Richard|last2=Wilds|first2=Claudia|date=February 1994|title=Little, Least and Saunders's Little Terns|url=https://britishbirds.co.uk/content/little-least-and-saunderss-little-terns|journal=British Birds|volume=87|issue=2|pages=60–66}}</ref>

== Taxonomy ==
Saunders's tern was first described in 1877 by [[British Empire|British]] [[botanist]] and [[ornithologist]] [[Allan Octavian Hume]] during his stay in [[British India]] as part of the [[Indian Civil Service|Imperial Civil Service]]. It was originally given the name black-shafted ternlet (''Sterna saundersi'') by Hume, which was later changed to its present name.<ref>{{cite book|author=Moulton, Edward |year=2003|chapter=The Contributions of Allan O. Hume to the Scientific Advancement of Indian Ornithology|editor= J. C. Daniel|editor2= G. W. Ugra |title=Petronia: Fifty Years of Post-Independence Ornithology in India|location= New Delhi, India|publisher= BNHS, Bombay & Oxford University Press|pages= 295–317}}</ref>

It is considered by some to be a subspecies of ''[[Sternula albifrons]]'', potentially of a larger species group, or otherwise conspecific. 

== Behavior ==

=== Feeding ===
The majority of the Saunders's tern's diet consists of various [[Marine life|marine]] animals, including small fish, [[Crustacean|crustaceans]], and [[Mollusca|mollusks]]. It will also eat several varieties of insects.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Almalki|first=Mohammed|date=March 2021|title=Breeding biology of Saunders's tern (''Sterna saundersi'') in the Farasan Islands, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia|journal=Saudi Journal of Biological Sciences|volume=28|issue=3|pages=1931–1937|doi=10.1016/j.sjbs.2020.12.044 |pmid=33732079 |pmc=7938151 |s2cid=232240844 |doi-access=free}}</ref> An individual will hover above the water for a sizable amount of time, before making a [[plunge dive]] to catch fish.<ref name=":0" />

== Status ==
It is a widely distributed species, and although population numbers are estimated to be small to large and even showing signs of decline, the species is not assessed as being close to the threshold of [[Vulnerable species|vulnerability]], and its [[conservation status]] has been assessed as "[[Least-concern species|least concern]]" on the [[IUCN Red List]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=[[BirdLife International]] |title=Saunders's Tern (''Sternula saundersi'') |url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/saunderss-tern-sternula-saundersi/text |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=BirdLife International datazone}}</ref><ref name="iucn"/>
